sentences.grammarNote:
**Mi nombre es** = My name is — uses **ser** (permanent identity).
Alternatively: **Me llamo Sean** (I call myself Sean) — the reflexive **llamarse** is more common in conversation.
Both are correct; **me llamo** sounds more natural in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: I have a lot of feelings.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tengo muchos sentimientos** — **tener** (to have) is used for many emotional and physical states in Spanish.
**Sentimientos** (feelings) is masculine plural — **muchos** agrees in gender and number.
The suffix **-miento** forms nouns from verbs: **sentir → sentimiento**, **pensamiento** (thought), **conocimiento** (knowledge).

sentences.base_language: When the New York Jets win I feel happy.
sentences.target_language: Cuando los New York Jets ganan, me siento feliz.
sentences.grammarNote: "Me siento" uses the reflexive verb 'sentirse' (to feel). Compare with 'soy feliz' (I am a happy person by nature) vs 'estoy feliz' or 'me siento feliz' (I feel happy right now).

sentences.base_language: When the New York Jets lose I feel sad.
sentences.target_language: Cuando los New York Jets pierden, me siento triste.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pierden** = they lose — **perder** (to lose) is an e→ie stem-changing verb: **pierdo, pierdes, pierde, perdemos, perdéis, pierden**.
**Me siento triste** = I feel sad — **sentirse** (reflexive) + adjective.
**Triste** has no gender change (same for masculine and feminine): **me siento triste**, **ella se siente triste**.

sentences.base_language: Watching the new york jets can make me feel a lot of things.
sentences.target_language: Ver a los New York Jets puede hacerme sentir muchas cosas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ver a** — the personal **a** is used here because the Jets are treated as a specific, personified group (a team you support).
**Puede hacerme sentir** = can make me feel — modal **poder** + infinitive **hacer** + indirect object **me** + infinitive **sentir**.
**Muchas cosas** = many things — **cosas** (feminine) → **muchas** agrees in gender.
**Alegría** = joy — from **alegre** (joyful), from Latin **alacer** (lively). The suffix **-ía** forms feminine abstract nouns from adjectives.

**Enojo** = anger, annoyance — from **enojar** (to anger). More common in Latin America; Spain often prefers **enfado**.
Related: **enojado** (angry), **enojarse** (to get angry — reflexive).

**Estrés** = stress — a loanword from English. Note the accent on the final syllable (stress falls there).
Adjective: **estresado/a** (stressed) — **estoy estresado** always uses **estar**.

**Emoción** = emotion, excitement — context determines which meaning applies.
The verb **emocionar** = to excite/move emotionally; **emocionarse** (reflexive) = to get excited / to become emotional.
Adjective: **emocionado/a** (excited/moved) — always with **estar**.

**Ansiedad** = anxiety — the suffix **-dad** forms abstract feminine nouns: **ansioso → ansiedad**, **feliz → felicidad**, **tranquilo → tranquilidad**.
Also common: **los nervios** (nerves/nervousness) — **tengo los nervios** (I'm nervous / I have the nerves).

**Ahora mismo** = right now (more emphatic than just **ahora**).
**Me siento tranquilo** — **sentirse** (reflexive) + adjective for how you feel at this moment.
Alternatively: **estoy tranquilo** — both work; **me siento** is slightly more introspective.

sentences.base_language: Because it is not football season
sentences.target_language: Porque no es temporada de fútbol americano
sentences.grammarNote:
**Porque** = because — one word, no accent (answers *why?*).
Contrast: **¿Por qué?** (two words, accent — asks *why?*).
**Fútbol americano** = American football — in Spain and Latin America, **fútbol** alone means soccer; you must add **americano** to specify the NFL sport.
